prompt:
Create `design/user-scenarios.md` — an escalating ladder of PROBLEM-SPACE user scenarios that will serve as the source of requirements for the-valley. First read the freshly restructured corpus on main: README.md, design/requirements.md, design/architecture.md, design/scenarios.md, design/roadmap.md, design/openquestions.md, design/contribute.md, design/verification.md. Match the voice: dry, confident, tables, no padding.

## Framing (put a short version of this at the top of the doc)
The docs are layered: premise (README) → requirements → architecture → design. This doc sits at the REQUIREMENTS layer: an escalating set of user scenarios, ordered by actors-and-trust, that requirements fall out of. It is problem-space — what a user experiences and needs, with ZERO mechanisms (no bus, no ref names, no attestation formats, no NATS). The existing design/scenarios.md is the complementary solution-space artifact (architecture walk-throughs in event notation); add one preamble sentence to scenarios.md marking that distinction and cross-link the two docs both ways. Scenarios get acceptance-level detail only when they become top-priority; each is fleshed out as we build toward it, never sooner. Right now ONLY S1 is detailed.

## The ladder (owner-approved shape — craft the prose, do not re-derive the structure)
A table of seven rungs, escalating along actors-and-trust, each with: id, scenario (one crisp problem-space sentence), what's anchored in reality (the owner's actual repos/workflows), and its knowledge-graph increment. The rungs:

- **S1 — My repos live on my infrastructure and I can never lose them.** Anchor: the-valley itself as pilot. Knowledge increment: issues/ideas/decisions live WITH the repo as plain files (v0 below).
- **S2 — I push, and seconds later it's integrated; I never wait for CI.** Anchor: daily solo work. Knowledge increment: none new.
- **S3 — I dispatch an agent; its change lands with the same guarantees, attributably, unsupervised.** Anchor: klaus, the owner's daily agent-orchestration tool. Knowledge increment: agents read/write knowledge nodes; dispatch-against-a-node replaces dispatch-against-a-GitHub-issue.
- **S4 — Builds, deploys, and notifications just happen; 'why did X occur' is answerable from one history.** Anchor: cosmo (NixOS infra), reel-life (deployed service). Knowledge increment: node mutations become observable events; a change can close an issue.
- **S5 — A second, semi-trusted human lands a change without me granting them anything platform-shaped.** Anchor: first collaborator. Knowledge increment: none new.
- **S6 — A bad deploy gets attributed, reverted, and remembered.** Anchor: reel-life regression. Knowledge increment: incidents auto-file knowledge nodes with attribution.
- **S7 — Strangers contribute safely.** Explicitly deferred, maybe never (matches requirements.md non-goals).

S2–S7 get ONE short paragraph each — scenario, why it matters, one-line note of the hardest thing it will demand. No acceptance criteria for them yet; say explicitly they get detailed when they become top-priority.

## S1 — detailed (the only rung with acceptance criteria)
Write S1 as: narrative (a few sentences of what daily life looks like when it holds), scope decisions already made, and CHECKABLE acceptance criteria. Bake in these owner decisions:

1. **Hosting-only scope.** Bare repos on the owner's existing tailnet box (classic-laddie); direct push to main is correct at this rung (no integrator yet — that pain is S2/S3's job to motivate). Mirror-first migration: dual-push with GitHub retained as a transitional mirror, canonical origin flips per-repo when confident.
2. **Durability requirement: 'pushed = replicated'.** RPO ≈ 0 for pushed work — every push lands in at least two independent locations within minutes, one of them offsite (Hetzner); a push is never in only one place. RTO relaxed: full restore from offsite within a day. A restore must actually be PERFORMED AND VERIFIED, not merely configured, before S1 counts as done. (Mechanism — git mirror vs ZFS send vs restic — stays an open question; cite the roadmap Phase 0 table rather than restating it.)
3. **Agents keep working — direct-push interim mode.** The owner's klaus agents currently work GitHub-PR-shaped. During S1, agents operate in a degraded direct-push mode: push a branch to classic-laddie, the owner reviews the diff and merges manually — the owner IS the integrator, by hand. This is deliberate: feeling that pain is the validation signal that motivates the integrator rung. S1 is NOT done if agent-driven development on the pilot repo has to pause.
4. **Knowledge v0 — a directory convention, not a system.** Issues/ideas/decisions live in-repo as markdown files with YAML frontmatter (type, id, status, title; edges later). Creating an issue is a commit; closing one is a commit; listing is ls; search is grep; history is git log. No indexer, no events, no validation — schemas are documentation until there's an integrator to enforce them. The pilot repo's open design questions and review findings become the seed content (as a follow-up, not in this PR).

S1 acceptance criteria (make them a checklist; refine wording but keep substance):
- the-valley's canonical origin is classic-laddie; GitHub is a mirror.
- Every push is present in ≥2 independent locations within minutes, verified by checking, not by config.
- One full restore from the offsite copy has been performed and verified.
- A week of real work (human AND agent) on the pilot repo without touching GitHub.
- A klaus agent change lands via direct-push mode end to end.
- At least one real issue is opened, worked, and closed as an in-repo node — including one agent dispatched against a node instead of a GitHub issue.
- The migration + restore runbook exists and is repeatable for the next repo.

## Integration with the corpus (light touch)
- Add `design/user-scenarios.md` to the README doc map (one line, before requirements.md).
- In design/requirements.md, add one sentence noting requirements derive from the user-scenario ladder and link it. Do NOT rewrite requirements.md — that re-derivation is a follow-up step.
- Do NOT touch design/roadmap.md — re-deriving the roadmap against these scenarios is explicitly a follow-up; say so in the PR description.
- All relative links must resolve.

## PR description
Two sentences on the method (escalating problem-space scenarios → detailed requirements → roadmap re-derivation), note S1-only detail is deliberate, list the named follow-ups (requirements rewrite, roadmap re-derivation, seeding knowledge v0 with openquestions + review findings). No AI/assistant attribution, no Co-Authored-By.

Open a PR.
